coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission carbon dots from gallic acid for selective turn off fluorescent detection of chlorpyrifos in wastewater
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Hardeep
Jain, Vicky
Renuka Jyothi, S.
Bhanot, Deepak
Kumari, Bharti
Verma, Meenakshi
Mutreja, Vishal
Bhattu, Monika
coredata.dc:description: Chlorpyrifos, a widely used organophosphate pesticide, is known for its severe environmental and health impacts, including neurotoxicity and endocrine disruption in humans and wildlife. Thus, it has become very important to detect the abovementioned pesticide. In this regard, yellow fluorescent carbon dots (N-GA CDs) were synthesized using gallic acid as a novel precursor through a hydrothermal process. The reaction parameters, including temperature, time, and precursor ratios, were systematically optimized to enhance the quantum yield and fluorescence behavior of the N-GA CDs. The resulting N-GA CDs exhibited distinct dual emission bands and were employed for the sensitive detection of chlorpyrifos.. The method demonstrated an impressive detection limit of 0.422 µM over a broad linear detection range of 1 to 100 µM. The detection is attributed towards the strong H-bonding of CDs with target pesticide along with other interactions like dipole-dipole, π-π and electrostatic interactions. This study underscores the potential application of these N-GA CDs in wastewater remediation, particularly for chlorpyrifos.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chlorpyrifos
Pesticide sensor
Fluorimetric detection
Turn-off

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: U.V. Visible absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of N-GA CDs.
Fluorescence emission spectra of N-GA CDs.
(a-b) HR-TEM (c) SAED pattern and (d) The histogram of particle size distribution for N-GA CDs.
(a) Full scan XPS spectrum of N-GA CDs. (b) The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s. (c) The high-resolution XPS spectrum of O1s. (d) The high-resolution XPS spectrum of N1s.

a) Screening of N-GA CDs against various pesticides; b) Titration of N-GA CDs with the subsequent addition of chlorpyrifos (0 μM–100 μM); and c) Linearity curve for the subsequent decrease in fluorescent intensity on increasing the concentration of CP; and d) effect of pH on sensing potential of N-GA CDs showing a stability over a pH range of 2–11 Furthermore, the effect of temperature and pH is also observed on sensing behavior of N-GA CDs towards chlorpyrifos. Subsequently, the sensor was found to be stable over a pH range of 2–11 and temperature variation of 25–60 °C (Fig. 7d). Moreover, to explore the selectivity of the prepared N-GA CDs were analyzed in presence of other potential interfering pesticides and interestingly no significant change has been observed in the emissive response of pure N-GA CDs and complex (N-GA CDs with chlorpyrifos) (Fig. 8).
Interference studies of different pesticides and metal ions on fluorescence emissive spectra of pure N-GA CDs (a) and complex (N-GA CDs+chlorpyrifos).
FTIR spectra of N-GA CDs (black) and N-GA CDs + Chlorpyrifos (red) illustrating an additional peak at 3420 cm−1, corresponds to H-bonding between host-guest molecule.
Schematic presentation of presentation of N-GA CDs.
Recent approaches for the detection of chlorpyrifos.

row.entry: Greener CDs derived from gallic acid

head.ce:title: Dual-emission carbon dots from gallic acid for selective turn off fluorescent detection of chlorpyrifos in wastewater

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chlorpyrifos, a widely used organophosphate pesticide, is known for its severe environmental and health impacts, including neurotoxicity and endocrine disruption in humans and wildlife. Thus, it has become very important to detect the abovementioned pesticide. In this regard, yellow fluorescent carbon dots (N-GA CDs) were synthesized using gallic acid as a novel precursor through a hydrothermal process. The reaction parameters, including temperature, time, and precursor ratios, were systematically optimized to enhance the quantum yield and fluorescence behavior of the N-GA CDs. The resulting N-GA CDs exhibited distinct dual emission bands and were employed for the sensitive detection of chlorpyrifos.. The method demonstrated an impressive detection limit of 0.422 µM over a broad linear detection range of 1 to 100 µM. The detection is attributed towards the strong H-bonding of CDs with target pesticide along with other interactions like dipole-dipole, π-π and electrostatic interactions. This study underscores the potential application of these N-GA CDs in wastewater remediation, particularly for chlorpyrifos.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the increase in population and industrial revolution, agricultural land has been decreasing at an exponential rate and food demand is up surging. Hence, to fulfill the demand, agricultural land is exposed to various kinds of pesticides and fertilizers to enhance the agricultural yield. Among all the pesticides, organophosphate pesticides are the most widely employed [5], and chlorpyrifos(CP), an organophosphate insecticide is extensively used worldwide [4,9,33]. The persistent use of agronomics has resulted in its presence in the soil and water which has deleterious effects on human health and causes environmental degradation [10,15]. Owing to its low solubility and readily distribution from aqueous to organic phases, it significantly causes neurological ailments, muscular dystrophy, respiratory disorder, and in severe cases, fatalities [30]. The permissible limits for CP residues for various commodities including fruits, vegetables, grains, and drinking water, range from 0.01 to 1 µg/l [51]. In this current work, our major focus is the removal of water from drinking water in which permissible limit was set to be 0.03 mg/L. Therefore, sensitive and selective detection of CP is highly desirable for human health safety and environmental protection requirements. Till now, various traditional analytical methods have been reported such as gas chromatography-mass spectrometry(GCMS–) [1,42,47,49], high-performance liquid-chromatography (HPLC) [3,17,21], and Liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [13,22,31], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [14,29], Diode Array Detection [34], electrochemical techniques [50] etc. Although all the above reported techniques provide a highly selective nature towards analytes with low detection limits, however, offers various disadvantages including i) require large quantities of reagents or samples; ii) costly sophisticated instrumentation; and iii) time-consuming analytic procedures. Furthermore, there is a requirement for simple, cost-effective, and sustainable probes to detect chlorpyrifos. To achieve the above goals, fluorescence sensing is an efficient technique owing to its spatiotemporal resolution and high sensitivity and selectivity.

However, in recent time studies, carbon-based nanomaterials have attracted the researchers' interest toward their new family member i.e., Carbon dots which have better water solubility, cost-effective and biocompatibility, photostability, and fascinating tunable fluorescence properties which further open the door of versatile and promising applications in bioimaging [44], drug delivery [28], chemical sensing as well as biosensing along with photocatalytic [19] and electrocatalysis [8,36,37,43,45,48]. In addition to this, the optical and fluorescence emission properties of CDs have become a focal point of researchers to do extensive research to unfold the chemistry behind it to make it beneficial for mankind as well as environmental protection [46,20]. However, the origin of the versatility of such photoluminescence of carbon dots is unclear yet. Despite that, it is believed that the main responsible factors for the photoluminescence property of CDs are the quantum effect, emissive traps, and frequent radiative recombination of excitons [22,38,52]. Owing to these fascinating properties of carbon dots motivate researchers to make such nanosensors that can selectively and sensitively detect the various analytes such as metals [18], organic pollutants, biomolecules, and biomarkers in living cells [2,7,26]. Herein, we developed yellow emissive N-doped gallic acid-derived carbon quantum dots (N-GA CDs) to detect chlorpyrifos organophosphate pesticide in the aqueous sample solution.

Gallic acid and dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich and other solvents such as H2SO4, ethanol, Quinine sulfate hexane, toluene, isopropanol, tetrahydrofuran (THF), ethyl acetate, dioxane, acetone, methanol, acetonitrile, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from SRL chemicals. All reagents were used as such without further purification. Ultrapure water (Milli-Q water) was used in all experiments.

UV–visible absorption spectroscopy was carried out by placing the CDs solution in a 1 cm quartz cuvette and measuring using a Shimadzu UV-3600 UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer at room temperature. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-7000 FL spectrophotometer at room temperature. FT-IR spectra were measured on a Nicolet Avatar 360 FT-IR spectrophotometer. Dynamic light scattering was performed on Malvern Zetasizer. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were recorded using a JEOL-2010 transmission electron microscope. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of the powder samples were achieved on a Philips X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (l ¼ 1.541°A).

To study the optical properties of synthesized N-GA CDs, the UV–vis and fluorescent spectra were recorded (Fig. 2 ). The UV–vis spectra of N-GA CDs exhibited two absorption bands at 265 and 288 nm owing to the presence of polyaromatic chromophores, which are associated with π-π* transition of aromatic domains with n-π* transition of surface states. Furthermore, the emission spectra were observed at an excitation wavelength of 288 nm, and two emission bands were observed at wavelengths 412 nm and 546 nm. The dual emission might have originated from the functional groups present on the surface of N-GA CDs such as hydroxyl, amine and carbonyl groups. This passivated surface facilitates the emission in the carbonized core. Secondly, the band at 546 nm is attributed to the defects or the wavelength-independent behaviour of nitrogen and oxygen-containing groups. The N-GA CDs also show a yellow fluorescence when exposed to UV light.

FTIR spectra of N-GA CDs was employed to identify the surface functional groups present on the surface of carbon core of synthesized carbon nanomaterial in detail (Fig. 3 .). The FTIR spectrum of C dots displays a characteristic broad band between 3489 cm−1, indicating the presence of amino and oxygen functional groups on their surfaces along with C–H and CN stretching vibrations at 2937 cm−1, 2893 cm−1 respectively [16]. However, a strong band at the wavenumber range 1500–1710 cm−1 is ascribed to the CO stretching frequency of -CONH, -COOH and -CHO groups [32]. Further, the CO and CC stretching vibrations were at 1659 cm−1 and the peak at 1387 cm−1 was associated with C–H and N–H bending vibrations as well as the peak at 1255 cm−1 for C–O bending vibrations. Lastly, the final and most important observation is the absorption peak in the fingerprint region (1000–400 cm−1) that is complicated and includes CO, CH, NH and OH bending vibrations [39].

The XRD pattern depicted in Fig. 4 . exhibited a weak broad diffraction peak centered at about 2θ=∼24.7° corresponding to the (002) facet of graphite lattice spacing, which suggested the N-GA CDs had an amorphous carbon phase owing to some nitrogen-containing functional moieties present on their surface [11,15,25,27].

The morphology and size range of prepared N-GA CDs were studied using HR-TEM analysis that revealed the formation of spherical and monodispersed nanoparticles in the size range of 0–30 nm, and the average size of the N-GA CDs was found to be 14.6 ± 5.8 nm as shown in Fig. 5 (a–d). Following the dialysis, the salt was removed and pure N-GA CDs of size ∼14.6 ± 5.8 nm were obtained in water. The TEM images reveal that the N-GA CDs are finely dispersed and mostly spherical with little or no agglomeration. However, the increase in size of pure N-GA CDs is most likely due to the formation of hydrogen bonds between the carboxyl groups present on the surface of the N-GA CDs as revealed by the FTIR analysis.

To investigate the elemental composition and surface oxidation state of the elements present in the N-GA CDs, an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis was carried out. The full-scan spectrum of prepared N-GA CDs (Fig. 6 a), depicted three major peaks at 283, 398, 531 eV, and, which correspond to K-shell characteristic peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s and, O 1 s respectively. The high-resolution scan of the C 1 s region on deconvolution showed the presence of carbon in four different chemical environments (Fig. 6b), at 282.4 eV, 283.7 eV, 285 eV and 285.5 eV corresponding to CO, CC/CC, CO, and CO-C respectively. The high-resolution scan of the O1 s region was deconvoluted into three peaks at 529.1 eV, 530.8 eV, and 534 eV (Fig. 6c.), corresponding to the -OH, CO, COH, and COC bonds [35,40,41] respectively. The N 1 s spectrum was further deconvoluted into two peaks at 397.9 eV and 400 eV (Fig. 6d.), attributed to CN and NH groups, respectively [40] which are in correspondence with already reported literature [8,24,40].

To assess the sensing capability of N-GA CDs for detecting organophosphate pesticides, a photoluminescence analysis was conducted. The N-GA CDs were initially tested against various pesticides, including atrazine, malathion, glufosinate ammonia, imidacloprid, methidathion, and chlorpyrifos. For this experiment, 100 µL of 1 mM solutions of each pesticide were combined with the N-GA CDs sensing solution, and the resulting changes in fluorescence were recorded. Among all the pesticides tested, only chlorpyrifos induced a significant shift in fluorescence emission and a new emissive band is observed at 350 nm with a decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 412 nm and 546 nm, suggesting a specific interaction between N-GA CDs and chlorpyrifos (Fig. 7a
). To further investigate the sensitivity and binding affinity of N-GA CDs toward chlorpyrifos, titration studies were performed. As illustrated in Fig. 7b, the gradual addition of chlorpyrifos from 1 µL to 100 µL led to a consistent increase in the fluorescence intensity of the N-GA CDs at 350 nm and a gradual decrease at 412 nm and 546 nm, respectively (Fig. 7b). After adding 100 µL of chlorpyrifos, maximum enhancement at 350 nm was observed. Fig. 7c displays the relationship between the normalized fluorescence signal and the varying concentrations of chlorpyrifos. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the equation:
LOD =
3 σ
s l o p e
where 'σ' represents the standard deviation, and 's' denotes the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD and LOQ for chlorpyrifos was found to be 0.422 µM and 1.27 µM, with a linear detection range from 30 to 120 µM. Moreover the binding constant is calculated using the B-H plot and was calculated to be 1.03×106 M−1 . The comparison of the the fluorescent based chlorpyrifos detection has been illustrated in Table 1

The detection of chlorpyrifos using CDs is owing to various kinds of interactions between the host and guest including i) electrostatic interaction due to the electron rich species present on the surface of N-GA CDs; ii) π-π interactions due to the presence of aromatic rings in chlorpyrifos which further lead to the energy transfer via Fluorescence energy transfer mechanism; iii) dipole-dipole interactions due to the presence of chlorine group along with the electrostatic interactions; and iv) H- bonding between the functional groups of N-GA CDs with the electronegative atoms present on chlorpyrifos. Thus, the presence of chlorine group in chlorpyrifos makes the interactions more stronger n comparison to other organophosphate pesticides and hence, the selectivity is observed in comparison to other pesticides. Furthermore, FTIR analysis is performed in order to find the interactive mechanism of N-GA CDs with chlorpyrifos which showed the appearance of an additional peak at 3420 cm−1, suggesting a strong H-bonding between OH, NH group of synthesized N-GA CDs with P
S group of chlorpyrifos. The experimental analysis illustrates that the H-bonding majorly contributes in the detection of chlorpyrifos (Fig. 9). The binding constant was calculated using Benesi and Hildebrand's graphical strategy (B-H plot). The binding potential was evaluated as association constant by considering the slope and intercept of B-H plot. The binding constant was found to be 1.03×106 M −1.

Real sample analysis was performed on different kinds of samples including river water, tap water, and RO water. In this regard, the collected samples were initially tested for the pesticide content as they did not contain the chlorpyrifos. Thus, further these samples were spiked with a known quantity of pesticide solution ranging from 400 to 600 nM and the recovery analysis was performed to measure the sensitivity of the CDs towards chlorpyrifos. Below LOD, no sensing was observed however, at concentration higher than LOD, recovery was observed to be above 97 %.
Real sample
Concentration of chlorpyrifos (nM)
Recovery of chlorpyrifos (nM)
Recovery of chlorpyrifos (%)
River Water 400 – Below LOD
500 488.1 97.62
600 583.3 97.1
Tap Water 400 – Below LOD
500 489.4 97.88
600 585.6 97.6
RO Water 400 – Below LOD
500 488.4 97.68
600 584.9 97.48

To recapitulate, highly fluorescent N-GA CDs were successfully synthesized using gallic acid as novel precursors. The yield and fluorescent behaviour of these N-GA CDs were optimized by varying reaction parameters, including temperature, time, molar ratios of the precursors, and the concentration of precursor. These N-GA CDs exhibit distinct dual emission bands, likely originating from their functional groups and defects occurred due to these functional groups. The synthesized N-GA CDs were applied for the detection of chlorpyrifos, a widely used OPP and highly harmful to the environment as well as living beings. Detection was achieved by monitoring the appearance of a new emission band at 350 nm and the quenching of dual emissive bands of the N-GA CDs fluorescence in the presence of chlorpyrifos. The quenching effect is likely due to the surface complexation during the interaction between the N-GA CDs and chlorpyrifos. The method achieved a low detection limit of 0.422 µM and quantification limit of 1.27 µM over a wide linear detection range of 1–100 µM. This study suggests potential applications for these N-GA CDs in wastewater remediation, specifically for detecting contaminants like chlorpyrifos. Furthermore, the tunable photoluminescence properties and sensitivity of these N-GA CDs could be leveraged for real-time monitoring of various pollutants in complex environmental matrices. Future research could explore the integration of these N-GA CDs into advanced sensor platforms, potentially leading to the development of portable and cost-effective devices for continuous environmental monitoring and the detection of a broader range of toxicants in wastewater.

Hardeep Kaur: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Vicky Jain: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis. S. Renuka Jyothi: Writing – review & editing. Deepak Bhanot: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Bharti Kumari: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Meenakshi Verma: Visualization, Validation, Supervision. Vishal Mutreja: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Conceptualization. Monika Bhattu: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chlorpyrifos degradation by bacillus Sp. Strain UPMB10 isolated from polluted environment: analysis and characterization of the metabolite by GC-MS
Case Stud. Chem. Environ. Eng.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Adams, N.H., R. Umar, S. Ishaya, O.D. Nweke, N.S. Ilyasu, A.H. Jagaba, S. Usman, and H.M. Yakasai. 2024. “Chlorpyrifos degradation by bacillus Sp. Strain UPMB10 isolated from polluted environment: analysis and characterization of the metabolite by GC-MS.” Case Stud. Chem. Environ. Eng. 9 (January): 100608. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cscee.2024.100608.
